skill.name: building-chat-widgets
skill.description: Build interactive AI chat widgets with actions, forms, entity tagging, composer tools, and server-handled widget updates.
skill.summary: Guides teams through interactive ChatKit widget patterns for agentic chat interfaces.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, dynamic import, weak cryptography, and critical combination alerts, but these are mostly Markdown code fence and inline backtick false positives. No prompt injection or malicious intent was found. Medium risk remains because examples show server-side action mutation and token-bearing MCP forwarding patterns that require validation and trusted endpoints.

medium_findings.title: Server Action Examples Need Payload Authorization
medium_findings.description: The server action examples read action.payload values and then update hidden context, confirm database items, or call do_action. These are legitimate widget patterns, but implementations must validate action type, sender, user permissions, and payload ownership before mutation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly connect action payloads to state changes. The code is instructional, so risk depends on how users implement authorization.

medium_findings.title: Token-Bearing MCP Forwarding Requires Trusted Endpoint
medium_findings.description: The local wrapper example forwards access_token to agent_ctx.mcp_server_url. This is valid for trusted internal MCP servers, but a user-controlled URL could expose credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token and URL are visible in the same call pattern. The source of mcp_server_url is not defined, so legitimate configuration is possible.

low_findings.title: Relative Fetch Example Should Encode Search Query
low_findings.description: The entity search example interpolates query directly into a relative URL. It is not exfiltration, but production code should encode the query and enforce server-side validation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch call is a same-origin relative request, which lowers impact. Direct interpolation still creates avoidable request parsing and validation risk.

low_findings.title: Backtick Command Execution Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static Ruby and shell backtick alerts point to Markdown fences, inline component names, and example code delimiters. No shell execution primitive is present in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation examples or inline Markdown text. They do not execute commands or construct shell strings.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import and Weak Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The dynamic import alerts identify normal Python import syntax in fenced examples. The weak cryptography alerts point to prose and component reference tables, not hashing or encryption code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines contain import lists and Markdown tables. I did not find cryptographic operations or JavaScript dynamic import calls there.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The scanner combined unrelated documentation patterns into a critical alert. I did not find one code path that combines code execution, network transfer, and credential access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious elements occur in separate instructional snippets. Only the MCP token-forwarding example remains a medium review item.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance alerts point to payload IDs, RequestContext examples, and checklist text. I did not find commands that inspect hosts, users, networks, or interfaces.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss application IDs, context objects, and validation checklists. They do not run reconnaissance tools or collect system metadata.

dangerous_patterns.title: Server Action Examples Need Payload Authorization
dangerous_patterns.description: The server action examples read action.payload values and then update hidden context, confirm database items, or call do_action. These are legitimate widget patterns, but implementations must validate action type, sender, user permissions, and payload ownership before mutation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly connect action payloads to state changes. The code is instructional, so risk depends on how users implement authorization.

dangerous_patterns.title: Token-Bearing MCP Forwarding Requires Trusted Endpoint
dangerous_patterns.description: The local wrapper example forwards access_token to agent_ctx.mcp_server_url. This is valid for trusted internal MCP servers, but a user-controlled URL could expose credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token and URL are visible in the same call pattern. The source of mcp_server_url is not defined, so legitimate configuration is possible.

dangerous_patterns.title: Relative Fetch Example Should Encode Search Query
dangerous_patterns.description: The entity search example interpolates query directly into a relative URL. It is not exfiltration, but production code should encode the query and enforce server-side validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fetch call is a same-origin relative request, which lowers impact. Direct interpolation still creates avoidable request parsing and validation risk.

content.value_statement: Text-only chat limits product workflows. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to build interactive widgets with actions, forms, mentions, and backend handlers.
actual_capabilities: Explains reusable widget templates with dynamic data and action payloads.
Shows client-handled actions for navigation, local state, and follow-up messages.
Shows server-handled actions that replace widgets and stream assistant responses.
Covers entity tagging, mention search, and entity click behavior.
Documents composer tool selection and backend routing by selected mode.
Lists common ChatKit validation errors and their fixes.
limitations: It provides implementation guidance, not a complete application scaffold.
Examples require adaptation to the user's ChatKit version and backend framework.
Security controls such as authorization and input validation must be added by the implementer.
It does not include automated tests for generated widget code.

use_cases.title: Add Actionable Chat Results
use_cases.description: Create result lists, detail buttons, and selection widgets inside an AI chat flow.

use_cases.title: Connect Chat Actions to Backend Workflows
use_cases.description: Route widget clicks to server handlers that update widgets and continue the conversation.

use_cases.title: Design Agent Modes and Mentions
use_cases.description: Add composer tools and entity mentions so users can steer agent behavior precisely.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Widget
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one interactive result list.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a ChatKit widget for a list of items. Include labels, icons, and one client-handled action for selecting an item.

prompt_templates.title: Add Client Actions
prompt_templates.prompt: Add client-side ChatKit action handling for navigation, local selection state, and a follow-up user message. Explain each action payload.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Server Actions
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement a server-handled widget action that validates the payload, checks ownership, replaces the widget, and streams an assistant response.

prompt_templates.title: Harden a Widget Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this chat widget flow for unsafe payload handling, missing authorization, malformed entity search, and unclear client or server ownership.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a task picker with clickable rows.
output_examples.output: A widget plan with list rows, selection payloads, a client action handler, and state update guidance.

output_examples.input: A team needs a form submission inside chat.
output_examples.output: A server action workflow that validates form data, updates the widget, and sends a follow-up assistant response.

output_examples.input: An app needs @mentions for articles and people.
output_examples.output: An entity search design with grouped results, click behavior, and backend conversion notes.

best_practices: Validate every action payload on the server before changing data or context.
Keep each action owned by either the client or the server, not both.
Encode search queries and restrict token forwarding to trusted internal endpoints.
anti_patterns: Do not trust widget payloads only because they came from the chat UI.
Do not wrap RequestContext when the handler already receives a RequestContext object.
Do not use action.arguments when the ChatKit action data is stored in action.payload.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build interactive ChatKit widgets for AI chat, including buttons, lists, forms, mentions, and action handlers.

faq.question: Does it generate a full chat application?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples that must be integrated into an existing ChatKit application.

faq.question: Can actions run on the client and server?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill explains when to use client handlers and when to use server handlers.

faq.question: Does it cover entity tagging?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes mention search, entity metadata, click behavior, and backend conversion guidance.

faq.question: What security checks are needed?
faq.answer: Server actions should validate payloads, verify permissions, and only forward tokens to trusted services.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
